channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Might Welcome Chinese Investment, but America Is Wary
item.description: A pledge for more Chinese investment could face backlash given longstanding national security concerns in the United States.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.media:description: President Trump on Thursday in Beijing. There has been speculation that Mr. Trump and his advisers are seeking to secure a major investment pledge from China on the trip.

item.title: Inside Jack Schlossberg’s Chaotic Campaign to Revive Camelot
item.description: Erratic behavior and staff turnover have colored Mr. Schlossberg’s bid for a House seat in New York, raising questions about his readiness for office.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Fandos
item.media:description: Jack Schlossberg, a Kennedy heir who is running to represent much of Manhattan in Congress, has churned through campaign staff at an unusual pace.

item.title: Strategist Tied to Becerra and Newsom Pleads Guilty in Corruption Case
item.description: Dana Williamson, a former political strategist for Xavier Becerra and a former aide to Gov. Gavin Newsom, appeared in federal court on Thursday.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:description: Dana Williamson pleaded guilty to three felony charges in federal court on Thursday.

item.title: Why the Migrant Child Crisis Is Roiling the California Governor Race
item.description: The Times broke the story that has become a dominant line of attack against Xavier Becerra, the Democratic front-runner. Here are five things to know about it.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Dreier
item.media:credit: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Xavier Becerra, the former secretary of Health and Human Services, is campaigning for California governor.

item.title: U.S. Set to Drop Charges Against Indian Billionaire Accused of Fraud
item.description: The decision came after a meeting in which a lawyer for the billionaire, Gautam Adani, made an unusual offer, according to people familiar with the matter.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Hong, Ben Protess, William K. Rashbaum and Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: M. Scott Brauer for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gautam Adani had been accused by federal prosecutors in Brooklyn of defrauding investors.

item.title: In a City of Big Dreams, Many Young Adults See a Cloudy Future
item.description: A bleak job market. Rising rents. Huge debt. In New York and other cities, traditional milestones of adulthood feel further away for some 20- and 30-year-olds.
item.dc:creator: Troy Closson
item.media:credit: Tessa Belle Dillman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Soban Ali moved to New York eager to make his way in a new city. Like so many other young adults, he discovered hardship and disappointment.

item.title: What Could One Banana Cost? $10? Maybe for You, Some Fear.
item.description: Grocery stores can use shoppers’ personal data to charge different customers different prices, a practice known as surveillance pricing. Lawmakers in New York are considering a ban.
item.dc:creator: Claire Fahy
item.category: Retail, Wholesale and Department Store Union

item.title: Life, Death and Rebirth in Nepal, the Land of the Buddha
item.description: Starting at the birthplace of Buddhism, a writer traces how its teachings spread across Asia, transforming the continent forever.
item.dc:creator: Aatish Taseer and Keerthana Kunnath

item.title: In Thailand, Buddhism Is Twinned With Political Power
item.description: In Thailand, where it’s the state-sanctioned religion, practices have proved to be surprisingly mutable.
item.dc:creator: Aatish Taseer and Tomoko Yoneda

item.title: How Taiwan Became a Refuge for Buddhism
item.description: A Sinicized form of the religion has been preserved on the island, where daily life itself now sometimes seems like an exercise in Buddhist practice.
item.dc:creator: Aatish Taseer and Maxime Fossat

item.title: Ishmael Reed Is Writing a Play About Elon Musk
item.description: Ishmael Reed, provocateur and playwright, has a few words for the billionaires of Silicon Valley.
item.dc:creator: David Streitfeld
item.media:credit: Christie Hemm Klok for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ishmael Reed, 88, an author who has frequently challenged American political culture, at his home in Oakland, Calif. He is writing a play about Elon Musk and other tech billionaires.

item.title: Saudi Arabia and U.A.E. Carried Out Secret Attacks in Iran, U.S. Officials Say
item.description: The Gulf Arab states have been grappling with how to deter Iran after the war made clear the limits of American security guarantees.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt and Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tehran, the capital of Iran, last week.

item.title: In Qatar, Energy Sector Damage Is Severe, and the Way Back Will Be Long
item.description: Iranian strikes and a blockade have paralyzed Qatar’s gas engine, creating a technical bottleneck likely to stall exports for years.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A liquefied natural gas production facility in Qatar’s Ras Laffan Industrial City. Qatar is one of the world’s largest exporters of L.N.G., but Iranian attacks have crippled its production.

item.title: A Fact Check of Trump’s Claims on Inflation and Gas Prices in Iran War
item.description: He has minimized soaring gas prices, rising inflation and the American economy’s need for the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Linda Qiu
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Gabriel V. Cárdenas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gas prices have risen about 53 percent higher than they were at the start of the war on Feb. 28.

item.title: Consumers Spent More in April Despite High Gas Prices
item.description: Retail sales rose 0.5 percent despite higher prices for gas, food and other goods. But there are signs consumers are under some strain.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember
item.media:description: Retail sales data on Thursday showed American consumers are continuing to spend even as gas prices go up and paychecks are not keeping up with inflation.

item.title: Hantavirus Doesn’t Spread Easily, but Officials May Be Downplaying Risks
item.description: The virus is clearly far less contagious than the coronavirus, scientists agree, but they have found cases where it spread among people without direct contact.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Passengers from the MV Hondius evacuated from the cruise ship this week in Tenerife, Spain.

item.title: Argentina Races To Find Origin of Hantavirus Outbreak
item.description: The scientific investigation into the origin of cruise infection has become entangled with international finger pointing.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola
item.media:description: A sign shows information on hantavirus at a hospital in Bariloche, Argentina. A patient with no known connection to the cruise ship is hospitalized there with hantavirus.

item.title: Will Her Daughter Be Safe at Pali High as It Rebuilds From LA Wildfires?
item.description: It’s a nightmare faced by families all around Los Angeles: After wildfire smoke blanketed homes, schools and offices with toxic chemicals, when is it OK to go back?
item.dc:creator: Emily Baumgaertner Nunn and Jenna Schoenefeld
item.media:credit: Jenna Schoenefeld for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pearl Abrahams, a senior at Palisades Charter High School in California, attended classes — along with 2,400 other students — in an abandoned department store while her school, burned in the Palisades Fire last year, was rehabilitated.

item.title: Trump’s Push to Keep Coal Plants Open Is Costing Hundreds of Millions
item.description: Costs have been mounting in the year since the Trump administration began directing aging coal plants to stay open.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.media:credit: Jim West/UCG/Universal Images Group, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The J.H. Campbell coal-fired power plant in West Olive, Mich., one of five coal plants in the United States that President Trump ordered to remain open. The plant has reported $180 million in expenses associated with running the facility since last May.

item.title: U.S. Abruptly Cancels Deployment of 4,000 Troops to Poland
item.description: It was unclear why Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth called off the deployment to Poland, which has close relations with the United States.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: U.S. soldiers in Poland in November. There are about 7,400 U.S. troops in Poland, and about 80,000 in Europe.

item.title: The Family Branding of Sean Duffy’s Road Trip Reality Show
item.description: A trailer heralds the latest spinoff from the Trump administration.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.category: The Great American Road Trip (TV Program)

item.title: Cecilia Flores Became the Voice of Mexican Mothers
item.description: One of the most prominent activists for Mexico’s disappeared recently found the remains of one missing son. Now she has turned her attention to finding the other.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner and Fred Ramos
item.media:credit: Fred Ramos for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cecilia Flores during a search for her missing son, Alejandro Guadalupe Islas Flores, in Juan José Ríos, Sinaloa, Mexico.

item.title: In Venice, the Passion of Life and the Ghost of Art
item.description: The 2026 edition of the world’s oldest art exhibition is a celebration of vitality — for better and indeed for worse.
item.dc:creator: Jason Farago
item.media:credit: Matteo de Mayda for The New York Times

item.title: I Don’t Think You Can Even Call This Hypocrisy
item.description: The secrets of one of the architects of the religious right are being revealed. One of the secrets is that they weren’t really secrets.
item.dc:creator: David French

item.title: In Some Other Universe, This Might Be Funny
item.description: When MAHA turns into HAHA.
item.dc:creator: Frank Bruni and Bret Stephens

item.title: Who’s Running in 2028?
item.description: We look at potential candidates for the nation’s top job.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton

item.title: Flag With Swastikas and Star of David Flown at N.Y.U., Police Say
item.description: The flag appeared on a university building in the heart of Greenwich Village during a graduation week event, and resembled the purple N.Y.U. banners flying on campus.
item.dc:creator: Claire Fahy
item.media:description: A flag with swastikas and a Star of David was taken down from a building on the New York University campus.

item.title: How an Image of Washington at Prayer Became a Touchstone for the Right
item.description: A scene inspired by the winter at Valley Forge has become more prominent in the Trump era, along with claims that the United States was founded as a Christian nation.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler

item.title: With Alex Murdaugh Conviction Reversal, South Carolina Braces for Retrial and More Scrutiny
item.description: The State Supreme Court cited a local court clerk in reversing the result of one of the most-watched murder trials of the last decade. It has raised painful memories in the state’s Lowcountry region.
item.dc:creator: Shaila Dewan and Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs
item.media:description: Becky Hill, the former Colleton County clerk of court, during her guilty plea last year.

item.title: Wes Streeting Resigns and Calls for Leadership Contest to Replace Starmer
item.description: The British health secretary stepped down on Thursday, issuing an excoriating statement on the leadership of Prime Minister Keir Starmer.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Wes Streeting walking on Downing Street, London, where he had a meeting with Prime Minister Keir Starmer, on Wednesday.
